questions.text: I feel that ignoring a person's pain in order to stay comfortable is something I would need to reconcile with my conscience.
I believe that the ability to remain emotionally uninvolved is what allows some professionals to save lives.
When I hear about systemic injustice, I feel a moral imperative to contribute to change, not just to be informed.
I tend to evaluate situations involving suffering by their facts and outcomes rather than by the emotions they generate.
I believe that the moral weight of another person's suffering should be enough to alter the course of my day.
I have found that I am most useful in emotionally charged situations when I do not allow myself to be drawn into the emotion.
I feel that a world where people took each other's suffering more seriously would be fundamentally better.
I think that emotional responses to suffering, while natural, should be secondary to strategic action.
I consider it a reflection of my values when I go out of my way to address suffering I encounter.
I believe that the clearest thinking happens when you can examine a human problem without feeling personally affected by it.
I feel that my moral compass is most engaged when I am confronted with another person's undeserved hardship.
I find that I can move through emotionally difficult conversations without carrying the weight of them afterward.
I believe that responding to suffering is not optional for someone who considers themselves a good person.
I think that empathic responses can cloud judgment in situations where clear analysis is what the suffering person actually needs.
I feel that the way I respond to the pain of others is the truest test of my character.
I am more effective when I treat emotional situations as requiring objective assessment rather than personal engagement.
I believe that recognizing the moral significance of suffering — even when it is inconvenient — is what separates compassion from sentiment.
I think that the ability to detach from individual cases of suffering is what allows systems and institutions to function fairly.
